title: Earth's atmosphere — where it came from
examTip: Early atmosphere: mainly CO₂ (from volcanoes), little O₂. Oceans formed as water vapour condensed. Algae photosynthesised → O₂ increased, CO₂ decreased. CO₂ locked away in sedimentary rocks and fossil fuels. Today: ~78% N₂, ~21% O₂, ~1% Ar, 0.04% CO₂.
introTips: The early atmosphere was very different from today — it was mostly CO₂ with very little oxygen at all.
The change from CO₂-rich to O₂-rich took billions of years, driven mainly by photosynthesis from early algae.
CO₂ was also removed by dissolving in the oceans and by being locked into sedimentary rocks (limestone) and fossil fuels.
Today's atmosphere composition: ~78% nitrogen, ~21% oxygen, ~1% argon, 0.04% carbon dioxide — memorise these percentages.
Questions often ask you to describe EACH stage of atmospheric evolution — link each change to its cause.
intro: The air you are breathing right now is the product of over 4 billion years of change. When Earth first formed, there was no free oxygen at all — the atmosphere was a thick blanket of carbon dioxide and water vapour, hostile to all animal life. The transformation to the oxygen-rich atmosphere we depend on was driven almost entirely by living organisms — specifically, tiny photosynthetic algae that pumped oxygen into the air as a waste product. Understanding how the atmosphere evolved helps explain where our fossil fuels come from, why CO₂ matters, and how life itself shaped the planet.

keyPoints.heading: The early atmosphere — volcanoes and CO₂
keyPoints.content:
When Earth formed approximately 4.6 billion years ago, intense volcanic activity released gases from the interior of the planet — a process called degassing.

The early atmosphere consisted mainly of:
• Carbon dioxide (CO₂) — the dominant gas, similar to the atmospheres of Mars and Venus today
• Water vapour (H₂O) — released by volcanoes; later condensed to form the oceans
• Small amounts of nitrogen (N₂) and methane (CH₄)
• Very little or no oxygen (O₂)

Evidence: The atmospheres of Mars and Venus (which had less geological and biological activity) are still mostly CO₂ today, which supports the idea that Earth's early atmosphere was similar.
writeBullets: Earth formed ~4.6 billion years ago — early atmosphere from volcanic degassing
Early atmosphere: mainly CO₂ and water vapour
Also present: small amounts of N₂, CH₄
Almost no oxygen (O₂) in the early atmosphere
Evidence: Mars and Venus atmospheres still mainly CO₂ today

keyTerms.def: The release of gases from the Earth's interior through volcanic activity; responsible for the early atmosphere
The atmosphere of early Earth, dominated by CO₂ and water vapour, with very little oxygen

keyPoints.memTip: Early Earth = volcanic, hot, CO₂ everywhere. Think of present-day Venus (850°C surface, 96% CO₂ atmosphere) — that was Earth 4 billion years ago.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say the early atmosphere was 'like today but with more CO₂.' Stress: there was NO oxygen — this is a fundamental difference, not just a difference in proportion.

keyPoints.heading: Formation of the oceans and removal of CO₂
keyPoints.content:
As Earth cooled, the water vapour in the atmosphere condensed to form liquid water — creating the oceans around 3.8 billion years ago.

CO₂ was removed from the atmosphere in two main ways:
1. Dissolved into the oceans: CO₂ dissolves in seawater to form carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), which then reacts with rocks to form carbonate and bicarbonate ions. These eventually form limestone (calcium carbonate, CaCO₃) — locking CO₂ into rock.
2. Used by organisms: Once life evolved, photosynthetic organisms (algae) used CO₂ to make glucose. When they died, their organic carbon was eventually buried and converted over millions of years into fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas).

Both processes removed CO₂ permanently from the atmosphere and stored it as rock or fossil fuel carbon.
writeBullets: Water vapour condensed as Earth cooled → oceans formed
CO₂ dissolved in oceans → carbonate ions → limestone (CaCO₃)
CO₂ used by photosynthetic organisms → buried → fossil fuels
Both processes permanently removed CO₂ from atmosphere
CO₂ now locked in sedimentary rocks and fossil fuels

keyTerms.def: Water vapour cooling to form liquid water; how the oceans formed as Earth cooled
Calcium carbonate sedimentary rock; formed from marine organisms that used dissolved CO₂ to make shells, locking carbon into rock

keyPoints.memTip: Two carbon sinks: ROCKS (limestone) and FUELS (fossil fuels). Both grabbed CO₂ out of the air and stored it underground.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students need to be clear that fossil fuels and limestone both represent stored CO₂ — this is important context for why burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ back into the atmosphere.

keyPoints.heading: The rise of oxygen — photosynthesis by algae
keyPoints.content:
Around 2.7 billion years ago, algae (and later, plants) evolved the ability to photosynthesise:

6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

Photosynthesis:
• Removed CO₂ from the atmosphere
• Released O₂ into the atmosphere

Over hundreds of millions of years, the oxygen produced by photosynthesis gradually built up. Initially much of it was absorbed by iron in the oceans (forming iron oxide deposits in rocks — evidence in the fossil record). Once the oceans were saturated, O₂ accumulated in the atmosphere.

This increase in O₂ allowed complex (aerobic) life to evolve, and eventually led to the ozone layer forming — protecting life on land from UV radiation.
writeBullets: Algae evolved ~2.7 billion years ago → began photosynthesising
Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂
CO₂ removed, O₂ added to atmosphere over billions of years
Initial O₂ absorbed by iron in oceans (banded iron formations as evidence)
Eventually O₂ built up in atmosphere → enabled complex aerobic life

keyTerms.def: The process by which plants and algae convert CO₂ and H₂O into glucose and O₂ using light energy; responsible for increasing atmospheric O₂
Ancient sedimentary rock layers containing iron oxide; evidence that early O₂ was absorbed by iron in the oceans before building up in the atmosphere

keyPoints.memTip: Algae = the original oxygen factory. They spent billions of years pumping O₂ into the atmosphere. Without them, there would be no breathable air — and no you.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Link photosynthesis to the atmospheric change clearly: CO₂ in → O₂ out. Students sometimes say algae 'produced nitrogen' — they did not; nitrogen built up slowly as a chemically unreactive residual gas.

keyPoints.heading: Today's atmosphere — composition and how nitrogen accumulated
keyPoints.content:
The modern atmosphere (approximate percentages):
• Nitrogen (N₂): ~78%
• Oxygen (O₂): ~21%
• Argon (Ar): ~1% (noble gas, very unreactive)
• Carbon dioxide (CO₂): ~0.04%
• Trace amounts of other gases (water vapour, ozone, etc.)

Why is nitrogen so abundant?
Nitrogen is very unreactive (the N≡N triple bond is extremely strong). It was released by volcanoes and by decomposition/denitrification by bacteria, and because it doesn't react readily with anything, it accumulated over billions of years while other gases were used up or locked away.

Argon (~1%) is a noble gas — completely unreactive. It has simply accumulated from radioactive decay of potassium in the Earth's crust.
writeBullets: Modern atmosphere: ~78% N₂, ~21% O₂, ~1% Ar, ~0.04% CO₂
Nitrogen: unreactive, accumulated as other gases were removed/used
Oxygen: built up due to photosynthesis by algae and plants
Argon: noble gas from radioactive decay of potassium in crust
CO₂ now very low — most locked in rocks and fossil fuels

keyTerms.def: The most abundant gas in today's atmosphere (~78%); very unreactive due to strong triple bond, so it accumulated as other gases were removed
Noble gas making up ~1% of the atmosphere; chemically unreactive, accumulated from radioactive decay in Earth's crust

keyPoints.memTip: 78-21-1-0.04: Nearly 80% nitrogen, just over 20% oxygen, ~1% argon, a tiny bit of CO₂. Learn these four numbers.
keyPoints.tutor_note: In exams, '1% other gases' is often sufficient to mention argon. The key numbers to memorise are 78% N₂ and 21% O₂ — these appear most frequently.

tables.title: Evolution of Earth's atmosphere — key stages
rows: Volcanoes release CO₂, H₂O vapour, N₂
Early atmosphere: mainly CO₂ and water vapour
Water vapour condenses as Earth cools
CO₂ dissolves in oceans; limestone begins to form
Complex aerobic life possible; ozone layer forms

0.question: What was the main gas in Earth's early atmosphere?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! The early atmosphere was dominated by CO₂ from volcanic degassing. There was very little or no oxygen. The modern high-oxygen atmosphere developed over billions of years due to photosynthesis.
0.wrongFeedback: The early atmosphere was mainly CO₂, released by volcanic activity. Oxygen was almost absent — it only built up slowly over billions of years as algae began photosynthesising.

1.question: How did oxygen levels in the atmosphere increase over time?
options: Algae (and later plants) evolved and released O₂ as a by-product of photosynthesis
Volcanoes released oxygen into the atmosphere
Oxygen was always present but was locked in rocks
Oceans released oxygen when water evaporated
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Photosynthesis by early algae (6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂) gradually released O₂ into the atmosphere over billions of years.
1.wrongFeedback: Oxygen increased because algae (and later plants) evolved photosynthesis — releasing O₂ as a by-product. This process took billions of years to build up the ~21% O₂ we have today.

3.question: What are the approximate percentages of nitrogen and oxygen in today's atmosphere?
3.correctFeedback: Spot on! Today's atmosphere is approximately 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen, plus ~1% argon and 0.04% CO₂.
3.wrongFeedback: The modern atmosphere is approximately 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen — not the other way round. Nitrogen is by far the most abundant gas, even though oxygen is the one we need to breathe.

commonMistakes: Saying the early atmosphere was 'similar to today but with more CO₂' — it was completely different; there was almost NO oxygen at all in the early atmosphere.
Attributing the rise in oxygen to plants rather than algae — algae evolved first and were responsible for the initial rise in O₂ over billions of years. Plants came much later.
Forgetting that nitrogen is ~78% of the atmosphere, not oxygen — oxygen is ~21%. Many students swap these two values.
Not explaining WHY nitrogen accumulated — it is because nitrogen is very unreactive (strong triple bond) and was not removed by any chemical process.
Saying CO₂ was 'destroyed' — it was not destroyed but removed and stored: dissolved in oceans → limestone, or used in photosynthesis → fossil fuels.
examTips: Describe the evolution of the atmosphere in stages: (1) volcanic CO₂, (2) water vapour condensed → oceans, (3) algae → photosynthesis → O₂ increases, (4) modern atmosphere.
Always link each change to its cause: CO₂ decreased because it dissolved in oceans AND was used in photosynthesis. O₂ increased because of photosynthesis.
For 'evidence' questions: Mars and Venus still have CO₂-rich atmospheres (supporting the early Earth model); banded iron formations show O₂ was initially absorbed by iron before building up.
Memorise the four key percentages: 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 1% Ar, 0.04% CO₂ — these are frequently asked directly.
revisionCardBullets: Early atmosphere: mainly CO₂ and water vapour (from volcanoes)
Almost no O₂ in early atmosphere
Algae evolved → photosynthesis → O₂ released, CO₂ removed
CO₂ also stored in fossil fuels (buried organisms)
O₂ built up over billions of years
Modern atmosphere: ~78% N₂, ~21% O₂, ~1% Ar, ~0.04% CO₂
Nitrogen accumulated because it is very unreactive
Evidence: Mars/Venus still CO₂-rich; banded iron formations
